summary:
The Act of Union of 1707 merged the Kingdom of England (including Wales) and the Kingdom of Scotland into the single Kingdom of Great Britain, creating a unified parliament at Westminster and establishing the political entity that would build the world's largest empire. The union had deep historical roots in the 1603 'Union of the Crowns' when James VI of Scotland inherited the English throne as James I, creating a personal union of the two kingdoms under one monarch but leaving them with separate parliaments and legal systems. The formal political union was driven by practical considerations on both sides: Scotland, economically ruined by the failed Darien Scheme (an attempt to establish a Scottish colony in Panama), needed access to English markets and the growing colonial trade; England needed to secure its northern border, particularly given the risk that Scotland might choose a different (Catholic Stuart) monarch under the Act of Settlement, potentially creating a hostile northern neighbor allied with France.

The merger of parliaments was deeply unpopular in Scotland, where many saw it as absorption by a wealthier neighbor — Scottish satirists called the negotiators who accepted English financial inducements 'bought and sold for English gold.' The union abolished the Scottish Parliament and gave Scotland 45 seats in the new British House of Commons and 16 representative peers in the Lords (proportional to Scotland's relative wealth). Scotland retained its distinct legal system (Scots law), Presbyterian church governance, and educational institutions — concessions that preserved Scottish cultural distinctiveness within the union. The [[event:2384|Jacobite Risings]] of 1715 and 1745 represented armed challenges to the union from Scottish Highlanders loyal to the exiled Stuart dynasty; the defeat of the [[event:2385|Battle of Culloden]] (1746) definitively ended the Jacobite threat and allowed the Highland culture to be systematically dismantled. Great Britain subsequently emerged as the dominant global power of the 18th century.
